comment: Both ontologies define a class labeled Administrator with the same lexical form and compatible meaning as an administrative person in the conference system.
Exact lexical match on Author; both represent persons who write/submit conference contributions.

Exact label match, but the modeled semantics differ: in CMT Conference is the conference entity, while in ConfOf Conference is a subclass of Working_event, closer to the conference event itself rather than the management object.
Exact lexical match on Person with broadly compatible semantics in both ontologies.
Exact lexical match on Paper; both denote a paper contribution, though CMT models Paper as a Document and ConfOf as a Contribution subtype.
ConfOf Member_PC is commented as Reviewer and structurally reviews contributions, making it a close semantic counterpart to CMT Reviewer, though Member_PC is specifically a PC member.
Both classes denote program committee members involved in reviewing; ConfOf Member_PC has reviewing and expertise restrictions consistent with CMT ProgramCommitteeMember.
ProgramCommitteeChair and Chair_PC are near-synonymous labels for the chair of the program committee, with highly compatible intended meaning.
CMT SubjectArea and ConfOf Topic both represent thematic subject matter used to classify papers/contributions.
CMT PaperAbstract is a kind of Paper document, while ConfOf Contribution is a broader contribution concept encompassing papers and other submission types; the object is broader than the subject.
Both denote chair roles in conference organization, but CMT ConferenceChair is not clearly identical to ConfOf Chair_PC, which is specifically the PC chair.
CMT Document includes paper and review artifacts, while ConfOf Contribution includes paper-like submissions; they overlap substantially but are not equivalent.
In CMT, Co-author is explicitly a subclass of Author. ConfOf has Author but no separate Co-author class, so CMT Co-author is reasonably modeled as a subclass of ConfOf Author.
Both datatype properties associate a Person with an email string; labels differ only by naming convention.
Both are naming/title properties, but domains differ strongly: CMT name applies to Conference or Person, while ConfOf hasTitle applies to Contribution. This is only a weak related match.
Both datatype properties capture the title of a paper/contribution and have compatible ranges and intended usage.
Both datatype properties named abstract denote the abstract text of a submission/contribution.
Both object properties link a paper/contribution to its author. The labels differ, but domains and ranges are closely aligned.
Both properties relate an Author to a Paper/Contribution they wrote, and both are inverse to the corresponding paper-to-author property.
Both properties connect a paper/contribution to its thematic area or topic; the structural role in each ontology is closely similar.

CMT acceptedBy is an object property linking a paper to an administrator, whereas ConfOf Reviewing_results_event is an event class about review outcomes. They are only loosely related through acceptance workflow and are not directly equivalent.
